subject: Social and Political Life– II
overall_learning_objectives: Understand how a shirt reaches the market
Learn about different people involved in production and trade
Recognize why prices and profits vary
Identify challenges faced by workers and traders
Develop observation, analysis, and storytelling skills

lesson_plan.title: Introduction: How a Shirt is Made
topics_covered: Journey of a shirt from cotton to finished product
Stages: cotton farming, ginning, spinning, weaving, tailoring
student_activities: Look at pictures showing cotton, yarn, cloth, and shirt
List the stages of production they notice
Share which stage seems most interesting

lesson_plan.title: People Involved in the Market
topics_covered: Roles of farmers, weavers, factory workers, traders, and sellers
How each contributes to making a shirt
teaching_strategy: Use images or props for each role
student_activities: Discuss who they think earns the most and why

lesson_plan.title: Costs and Prices
topics_covered: Why prices increase from farmer to market
teaching_strategy: Draw a small price chain on board
student_activities: Draw a simple diagram showing price increase

lesson_plan.title: Challenges and Fairness
topics_covered: Difficulties faced by workers and farmers
Basic concept of fairness in markets
teaching_strategy: Discuss simple questions: Is it fair? Why or why not?
student_activities: Share one challenge a worker or farmer might face
Suggest one way to make it fairer

lesson_plan.title: Real-Life Connection
topics_covered: Linking chapter ideas to students’ own shopping experiences
teaching_strategy: Show a simple map or chart of local shops
student_activities: List items they buy and how they reach the store
Discuss similarities with shirt production journey

lesson_plan.title: Revision and Fun Quiz
topics_covered: Recap of shirt journey, people, prices, and fairness
teaching_strategy: Class discussion connecting past and present
student_activities: Draw or share one new thing learned

teaching_aids: Pictures showing cotton farming, weaving, tailoring, and shops
Simple props for farmers, weavers, traders
Charts and maps for visual learning
teacher_tip: Keep the lesson visual and interactive. Use stories, diagrams, and real-life examples. Encourage students to relate the market chain to things they buy and observe.
